areas where player character may face diplomatic or combat opposition
state-level military threat based on protagonist faction and storyline

safety_signals.visual_form: ports displaying player faction colors and insignia
safety_signals.meaning: safe harbor for trade, recruitment, and mission advancement

safety_signals.visual_form: ports marked as invested_in or influenced by player
safety_signals.meaning: player-controlled commercial safe zones reducing threat level

safety_signals.visual_form: regional sea zones associated with player's home nation
safety_signals.meaning: lower encounter rates with hostile entities

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Portuguese ports and territorial waters in Atlantic and Indian Ocean routes

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Spanish Armada visual representation; Catalina Erantzo as former Spanish naval officer turned antagonist
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Spanish colonial ports and Caribbean/Atlantic territories

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: English ports and North Atlantic waters

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mediterranean and Eastern trade route ports

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dutch trading posts and Northern European ports

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mediterranean ports and Italian commercial centers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: period-appropriate ship designs, port architecture, and costume presentation reflecting historical 1500s maritime aesthetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPC merchants, naval officers, and rulers conduct trade and diplomacy according to historical nation-state rivalries

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Portuguese and Spanish faction territories display distinct regional control in Atlantic and colonial routes; visual delineation of Treaty of Tordesillas boundary
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: protagonist encounters differ based on faction: Portuguese protagonists receive royal missions; Spanish characters face Franco family antagonism

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: protagonist visual representation changes through title advancement; increased crew and ship size reflect growing status
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs react with increased deference; port authorities grant access to restricted areas; higher-tier missions become available

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: invested neutral ports display player faction colors and banners; visual port amenities reflect investment level
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player gains economic benefits and safe harbor in controlled ports; neutral zones transition toward player sphere of influence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: high-encounter-rate regions show increased enemy ship sprite frequency; damaged port structures may indicate contested areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: merchant routes become dangerous; naval patrols increase; random combat encounters escalate in specific regions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: protagonist appearance and starting equipment reflect chosen career (explorer, pirate, privateer, cartographer, treasure hunter, merchant)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: available missions, NPC interactions, and storyline paths diverge based on career; each of six protagonists follows distinct narrative arc

environmental_storytelling.example: Major European ports (Lisbon, Seville, Venice) display larger, more detailed architecture and higher NPC density than remote neutral ports, communicating strategic importance and trade centrality

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Regional seas and coastlines associated with specific nations through color coding, flag placement, and port insignia patterns

environmental_storytelling.example: Period-appropriate caravel and galleon designs communicate historical setting; player ship upgrades visually manifest progression from small merchant vessel to large fleet flagship
Atlantis discovery narrative in João Franco storyline uses visual map markers and environmental anomalies to signal unexplored regions and quest objectives

Protagonists' starting costumes reflect national origin (Portuguese noble, Spanish officer, English privateer, Dutch scholar, Italian merchant, Turkish merchant); NPC apparel similarly encodes faction affiliation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: assigns exploration and nobility-advancement tasks to Portuguese protagonists; grants titles and increases reputation with royal commissions
pursue protagonist through narrative; Catalina Erantzo hunts Franco family members; Spanish Armada blocks certain trade routes
congregate in major ports; offer commodity exchange and price fluctuation based on supply/demand; avoid high-danger waters
patrol contested waters and neutral territories; attack merchant vessels; Catalina Erantzo pirate career allows player assumption of this role
conduct regular maritime patrols in faction-controlled waters; react hostilely to foreign or pirate vessels; facilitate privateering contracts
grant access to previously restricted services and missions after player investment; display increased friendliness toward invested-port owners

notes: Uncharted Waters: New Horizons communicates world-state primarily through protagonist career selection and faction-based narrative branching rather than dynamic visual world transformation. The game's Age of Discovery setting provides historical authenticity through period-accurate ship designs and port architecture. Manifestation of danger/safety operates through region-specific encounter mechanics and port control systems rather than explicit visual hazard indicators. The six-protagonist system creates faction-aligned storytelling where visual/behavioral communication shifts based on chosen character, making the game's communication paradigm heavily narrative-dependent. T3 tier data constraints prevent specific visual asset enumeration; analysis based on gameplay mechanics and historical game documentation.
